ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Chilli Gobi (battered fried cauliflower in chilli sauce). FOODON_03301710 (cauliflower food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Cauliflower) used as closest parent; no specific code exists for the battered, fried, and sauced preparation known as Chilli Gobi.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Chilli Gobi as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Chilli Gobi is approximately 200 g, comprising battered deep-fried cauliflower florets tossed in a spiced chilli-soy sauce with capsicum and spring onions.

nutrient_source.title: Gobi Manchurian / Chilli Cauliflower — composite estimate derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) cauliflower raw values and USDA FoodData Central entries for batter-fried vegetables and chilli-soy sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Chilli Gobi as a prepared dish. Values estimated by combining ICMR-NIN cauliflower raw data (per 100 g edible portion: energy 30 kcal, protein 2.6 g, fat 0.4 g, carbohydrate 4.0 g, fibre 1.2 g) with adjustments for deep-frying in batter (oil absorption ~8–10 g fat per 100 g final product, batter adding ~15–18 g carbohydrate per 100 g final product) and chilli-soy sauce contribution (sodium ~300–400 mg per 100 g final product). Final values represent a reasonable central estimate for a restaurant-prepared Chilli Gobi. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of battered fried vegetables and mixed vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on battered/fried cauliflower and starchy sauce components; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chilli Gobi. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant glycemic drivers: wheat-flour and cornflour batter (GI ~65–70 for refined flour products) moderated by the cauliflower base (low GI vegetable, GI ~15) and fat content from deep-frying which slows gastric emptying. A blended GI of approximately 55 is defensible for this batter-fried, sauce-coated vegetable dish, classifying it as low GI. GL per 200 g serving = (55 × 18.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 20.4 g. Source: Atkinson et al. 2008 International GI Tables, University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and chilli sauce (~380 mg per 100 g; ~760 mg per standard 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Chilli Gobi to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium soy sauce preparations, as a single serving can contribute approximately 33% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) driven by refined wheat and cornflour batter; glycemic load of ~20 per 200 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Chilli Gobi in small portions due to its low GI and moderate glycemic load, and pair it with high-fibre foods or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat from deep-frying (~9.2 g per 100 g) and significant sodium load; however, saturated fat is relatively low (~1.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol is negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Chilli Gobi should be eaten sparingly due to its deep-fried fat content and high sodium; choosing air-fried or baked versions and requesting reduced-sauce preparations would meaningfully lower cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Gobi is a popular Indo-Chinese vegetarian dish consisting of cauliflower florets coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour and cornflour, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a bold sauce made from chilli sauce, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, green chillies, and capsicum, garnished with spring onions. The dish originates from the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition that emerged in Kolkata, India, and has become widely popular across Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The texture contrasts a crunchy exterior with a tender cauliflower interior, and the flavour profile is spicy, tangy, savoury, and slightly sweet. Nutritionally, Chilli Gobi is a moderate-energy dish (~165 kcal per 100 g) with significant fat from deep-frying (~9.2 g), moderate carbohydrate from the batter (~18.5 g), and modest protein (~3.8 g). The sodium content is elevated (~380 mg per 100 g) due to soy and chilli sauces, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at low (~55) owing to the refined flour batter, and the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 20 g. Cauliflower contributes dietary fibre (~2.1 g per 100 g), vitamin C (~22 mg), folate (~38 mcg), and potassium (~210 mg). The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, but contains gluten and soy allergens. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Indian vegetarian restaurants and is a staple of the Indo-Chinese menu in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chilli Gobi?
What is the glycemic index of Chilli Gobi?
Is Chilli Gobi high in sodium?
What are the ingredients in Chilli Gobi?
Is Chilli Gobi the same as Gobi Manchurian?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Chilli Gobi as a prepared restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN cauliflower raw data with USDA FoodData Central data for batter-fried vegetables and chilli-soy sauce components. GI is an analogous estimate based on the International GI Tables (Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is transparent, the component sources are approved, and the resulting values are internally consistent and plausible for this dish type.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry for Chilli Gobi as a prepared dish exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values should be updated if a direct measurement becomes available. 2. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA component sources used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA data for all components. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as cauliflower and the other plant-based ingredients in this dish contain no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as vegetable oils used in commercial frying in Singapore are predominantly low in trans fats; confirmed negligible per USDA data. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 7. GI estimated at 55 (low) using analogous-estimate methodology; glycemic_load_per_serving calculated as (55 × 18.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 20.35, rounded to 20.4 g for a 200 g serving. 8. The food_name has been cleaned from the input '❤️💋Chilli Gobi❤️' to the canonical English form 'Chilli Gobi'. 9. The cuisine field in the input was the restaurant name; corrected to 'Indian' based on the restaurant's cuisine type. 10. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in a Singapore Indian restaurant.
